summary: Largest-cord-count Incahuasi khipu. 490 cords total (138 L1 + deep subsidiaries to L5), NB-dominant (232/490 = 47%), 2+-ARBRE. v3 unlocks 3 vocabulary occurrences (v1: 1): qaqa x1 (on L2!), kaki x1 (Pleiades/dried-corn), waki x1 (hapax). 12 Ascher sums, 0/12 verified (LOW integrity). Cross-khipu audit test vs UR278 row totals: 0 matches -- UR262 is NOT a UR266-style cross-auditor. 8 occurrences of value 10 but only 6% density (not a positional-constant signature; UR267B had 25% density). The khipu is a MASSIVE BULK DATA STORE with extremely sparse textual annotation -- the biggest accounting document in the analyzed Incahuasi set but also the most minimally labeled.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  KH0498 = UR262. 490 cords total: 138 L1 + 239 L2 + 79 L3 + 29 L4 + 5 L5. Architecture: 2+-ARBRE, schema STRUCTURED depth L5.
  9 clusters (reasonable number given cord count -- average 54 cords per cluster).
  5 colors: NB(232, 47%), AB(181, 37%), KB(28, 6%), MB(27, 6%), GG(8, 2%). NB-dominant.
  Excavation: Incahuasi. OKR notes: 'observations and drawings attached' (minimal, no specific UE).
  Not individually discussed in Urton 2017 despite being the largest Incahuasi khipu by cord count.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Most cords resolved; 8 L? remain.
  v1 -> v3: 1 -> 3 STRING readings (+2), 1 -> 3 distinct vocabulary (+2). Modest gain.
  Old v1 called this 'only 1 STRING (qaqa), the universal label, rest is numbers'. v3 adds kaki and waki but the document remains minimally textual.

ASCHER LAYER
  12 pendant-pendant sums parsed. Max cascade depth = 2.
  Verified checksums: 0 / 12 (0%). LOW integrity -- consistent with day-book/raw-data tier, NOT audit.
  covering_ratio: LOW.
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0.
  The 0/12 is similar to UR267A (0/13) and the day-book pattern. But UR262 has no '15' or '47' positional constant density to confirm day-book role. The 0/12 likely reflects parsing difficulty on a very large document rather than deliberate deferred integrity.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Largest cord count (490) of the analyzed Incahuasi set.
  Deep L5 tree (shared with UR280's L5 depth) = hierarchical.
  9 clusters × 54 cords/cluster average = substantial sections.
  No standard positional constant dominates:
    - '10' appears 8 times (6%) -- below the 20%+ audit threshold.
    - '47' appears 1 time (incidental).
    - No '15' signature.
  Top repeated values: 10 x8, 51 x3, 53 x3, 210 x2, 200 x2. No value dominates.
  Grand total L1 = 24,987. If subsidiaries are added the total is much larger.

VOCABULARY (3 words, 3 occurrences, v1-syllabic on KFG-merged knots, 100% dict hits)
  qaqa x1 (rock / landmark) -- AT L2 (not L1), likely anchoring a sub-tree rather than the main register
  kaki x1 (dried corn / Pleiades)
  waki x1 (HAPAX -- no standard gloss; possibly a proper name)
  Signature check:
    - Astronomical: kaki alone is insufficient (need mama, qaqa-as-dark-constellation, maqa, kama, paka, etc.). Not astronomical.
    - Cadastral: qaqa only (1 of 8 canonical markers). Not cadastral.
    - Juridical: absent.
    - Work register: if kaki = dried corn (rations), this is the ONLY work-register marker. Insufficient.
  MINIMAL vocabulary signature -- classifier's 'numerical_record' is correct.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p1 L2 MB qaqa: on an L2 cord (subsidiary), not L1. Anchors a sub-tree under cord 1.
  p49 L1 NB kaki: dried corn / Pleiades marker
  p84 L1 NB waki: hapax (possible proper name)

COLOR ROLE MAP
  NB (232, 47%) = PRIMARY DATA + text (2 of 3 vocab on NB).
  AB (181, 37%) = SECONDARY DATA.
  KB (28, 6%) = structural markers.
  MB (27, 6%) = phase separator -- carries the qaqa at p1 L2.
  GG (8, 2%) = minor identity/justice markers.
  NB + AB balance (47% + 37% = 84%) = binary-dominant register.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR262 is a VERY LARGE NUMERICAL REGISTER -- the biggest cord-count document at Incahuasi -- with only 3 textual cords and 0/12 Ascher sums verifying. It is neither a standard audit (fails integrity test) nor a cross-khipu auditor (fails UR278 match test) nor a positional-constant document (fails 10/15/47 density test). The minimal vocabulary + deep tree + huge bulk is consistent with a BULK DATA REPOSITORY: 490 cords of commodity counts organized into 9 deep subtrees, each anchored by very sparse labels. The function is probably what Urton would call a 'mother khipu' (khipu madre) -- a comprehensive master register that other khipus sub-catalogued. The lack of labels means the data was keyed to an external referent (another khipu, an oral memory, a clay tablet) rather than self-labeled.

THE STORY
  It is the biggest khipu that came out of Incahuasi. Four hundred and ninety cords hanging from a single primary, nine deep clusters, five levels of subsidiary branches. The largest data object the garrison kept, and almost no words at all.

  The opening is on medium-brown: qaqa, the rock, on a subsidiary cord of cord one. A single landmark label anchors the first sub-tree. Then forty-eight cords of numbers on near-black and alpaca-brown, the scribe counting in silence.

  At cord forty-nine, on near-black, the only labeling word in the main register: kaki. In an astronomical khipu this would be the Pleiades; in a commodity khipu this is dried corn. Which register is UR262? Without more vocabulary, it is impossible to say for certain.

  Thirty-five cords later, at cord eighty-four, on near-black again: waki. A hapax -- we have not glossed it reliably elsewhere. If it is a proper name, it marks an individual (a kuraka, a witness, a responsible party). If it is a syllabary reduction of an unknown Quechua or Aymara root, its meaning is open.

  After cord eighty-four, silence. More numbers. Four hundred more cords of numerical subdivision. Twelve pendant-pendant sums are declared across the document. None of the twelve verifies under machine parsing in 2026. Either the Ascher arithmetic was not the primary integrity mechanism here, or the sums were parsed incorrectly by the cataloguers, or the khipu was never balanced. The LOW verification rate is consistent with the DAY-BOOK tier in the standardized Inca audit format -- but UR262 does not carry the positional constant that would confirm it as a day-book.

  What UR262 IS, without ambiguity, is SIZE. It is the largest cord object excavated at Incahuasi. Whatever it counted, it counted a lot of it, and it organized the counts in five levels of hierarchy for someone who could read the tree. The minimal labeling suggests the reader already knew what the document concerned: maybe a regular delivery, maybe the garrison's total inventory, maybe a ritual offering cycle. The document PRESUMES its context.

  And when the empire fell, UR262 was left in the archive like the others, its four hundred and ninety cords knotted and preserved, its context forgotten. Five hundred years later, we can count the cords, map the tree, identify three labels, and then stop -- because the khipu does not tell us more.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening (p1 + L2 MB qaqa): rock-anchor subsidiary under cord 1.
  Section A (p1-p48): numerical, no L1 vocabulary.
  Section B (p49-p83): kaki at p49, then numerical.
  Section C (p84-p138): waki at p84, then numerical.
  L1 end (p138) + deep subsidiaries: 352 more cords in the tree.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Large-cord-count khipus are rare in the OKR and typically underanalyzed. UR006 (Leymebamba, 874 cords astronomical) is the corpus-largest; UR262 (490) is the Incahuasi-largest.
  'Mother khipu' (khipu madre) is attested in colonial sources (Calancha 1638, Murua 1590) as a comprehensive register from which daughter khipus (kipu hijos) were extracted. UR262's scale and minimal labeling are consistent with this functional role, though the specific content is unidentifiable without further context.
  The 0/12 Ascher verification could reflect a khipu-specific scribal convention, a parsing issue with the KFG sum declarations, or a deliberate choice to defer integrity to an external audit. Extending the cross-khipu audit test to other Incahuasi khipus (does UR266 audit UR262?) is a research step.

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR006 (Leymebamba, 874 cords astronomical) -- LARGER PARALLEL: at corpus level the largest known. UR262 is the Incahuasi max.
  UR278 (UE 16 astronomical calendar) -- NO CROSS-MATCH (0/65 value matches to row totals). UR262 does not audit UR278.
  UR280 (UE 10 Kallanka) -- shared L5 depth; UR280 has 250 cords, UR262 has 490 -- different scale but similar deep-tree structure.
  UR266 (UE 12 cross-khipu audit) -- possible candidate to audit UR262 if there are matching cord-total or subtree-sum patterns; not yet tested.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 L2 MB qaqa: single subsidiary-level label.
  p49 L1 NB kaki: first main-register vocabulary.
  p84 L1 NB waki: hapax at the text's midpoint.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  490-cord count (largest at Incahuasi): reproducible from v3.
  0/12 sums verified: reproducible.
  L5 depth: reproducible.
  Could UR266 audit UR262? Test: for each of UR262's 9 cluster totals, check if they appear as values in UR266. Not yet run.

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (3/3 dict-confirmed).
  Document type: MEDIUM (numerical_record is correct; specific commodity/function unknown).
  Narrative: MEDIUM (consistent but underspecified; the 'mother khipu' hypothesis is a plausibility rather than a proof).
  Data integrity: LOW-MEDIUM (0/12 verified, 8 L? remain).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Only 1 STRING (qaqa)' -- UPDATED: 3 STRING readings, 3 distinct words.
  - 'Largest Incahuasi khipu by cord count' -- CONFIRMED (490).
  - Confidence 'low' -- RAISED to medium.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 490 cords (confirmed).
  - 2+-ARBRE depth L5 (confirmed).
  - NB dominant (47%) (confirmed).
  - 115 EMPTY cords (confirmed).
references: Sivan, J. (2026). Deep read UR262 v3 -- contributions/validated/UR262.json (this record).
Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Ascher Sums as Erasure Codes. ALBA Project preprint.
Urton, G. & Chu, A. (2015). Accounting in the King's Storehouse: the Inkawasi Khipu Archive. LAQ 26(4).
Urton, G. (2017). Inka History in Knots. Univ. of Texas Press.
Calancha, A. de la (1638). Coronica moralizada del orden de San Agustin -- khipu madre / kipu hijos reference.
Murua, M. de (1590). Historia general del Peru -- khipu hierarchy.
